_note: VERANHOLM — a living city dived through the scope tree, rendered top-down at every scale. city > district > block > building > floor > room > occupant. LAZY: each level materializes on reveal (walk into any building). Building TYPE is real data (use_roll → doctor/shop/residential/school/cafe/office in the renderer). Occupants MOVE between rooms: each floor chains its rooms with a `door` edge, and occupants `move` along it + mingle with whoever is co-located. Nothing exists until observed; folds back when you leave.
_natural_detail_note: LOD DETAIL FIELDS (wooded/grassy/dry, inside `fields`) — the same two-axis logic (form × where) that drives biomes, one level finer. They say WHERE small natural features want to sit; the block→grove/deadpatch generators scatter entities onto them (where-gated), so the detail at block zoom is CONTINUOUS with the biome seen from region zoom — a forest block sprouts groves, a dry fringe shows dead grass. Pure region algebra; the engine never knows what a tree is. (Note lives OUTSIDE `fields` — every key inside `fields` is parsed as a formula, and an em-dash in a formula is a lex error.)
_fields_note: LAYERED WORLD as composed fields (region algebra). TWO INDEPENDENT AXES, both pure data: (1) TERRAIN FORM — what the land IS (water/beach/grass/forest/desert/rock/snow), from elevation + an independent low-frequency `moisture` field; (2) BUILDABILITY — can you build here. These are DECOUPLED on purpose: a forest or a desert is BUILDABLE (you clear trees / build on sand), but steep rock, high mountains, snow and water are NOT. So 'unbuildable' always has a visible reason on the map. Biome noise is LOW-FREQUENCY (big scale) → coherent contiguous regions, never salt-and-pepper. The engine only composes regions; it never knows what a 'forest' means.
_plot_land_note: ROADS vs PLOTS — this is SUBTRACTION, not fronting. `plot_land` = buildable_block MINUS a band around every road (`road_near`), so buildings simply can't overlap a lane; plots carve from what's left. This is the SUBURBAN/RURAL form (houses avoid the road, scatter on remaining land). The CITY form is different — there plots would FRONT the road (setback + a frontage row along the street, interior lots behind), which is a stronger 'plots orient to the road' rule NOT built yet. Both are authorable as data (the engine subtracts roads via road_near; fronting would be a future partition option that reads 'which road do I face'). Kept subtraction as the base on purpose — city vs suburb is a DEV's choice, not a baked-in law. road_near width (0.014) = the corridor half-tuning; widen for wider streets.
_buildable_block_note: THE FINER RULE (scope decision, no engine change): building plots sit on a STRICTER mask than blocks do — `buildable_block` = buildable MINUS a coast setback (`near(water,0.035)`) MINUS a stricter local slope (`steeper`), so lots read as tight urban plots set back from the water, not 'anywhere on land'. Pure region algebra; sampled through the BLOCK's fractal patch, so the setback is measured against THIS block's real coastline at every seed — one formula, correct everywhere. Authoring MEANING; the engine only composes the regions. (NOTE: comment keys must live OUTSIDE `fields` — every key inside `fields` is parsed as a formula.)
_routes_note: City districts are VORONOI (organic territory), so they need real terrain-aware ROADS routed between them (around water, bridging). Districts route LANES between blocks (the arteries passing through). But BUILDINGS are not routed: the block→building `street` route was REMOVED on purpose — it double-drew a road network buildings had to dodge. Inside a block, circulation is EMERGENT: plots carve from `plot_land` (buildable minus the through-lanes via `road_near`), and the GAPS between the subdivide plots ARE the local streets. Construction determines block roads, not a separate routing pass. Two directions of causality: ABOVE the block road→land (artery cuts buildable); INSIDE the block land→road (plots make streets).

routes.cost: 1 + 8*field(water, fx, fy) + 6*field(steep, fx, fy) + 12*field(rock, fx, fy)

routes._note: FRACTAL CIRCULATION: `gates:true` includes the crossing points the CITY road left on this district (engine `gate` children) as lane nodes — so the lane web CONNECTS to the artery where it enters, instead of overlaying it blindly. The lanes are the ARTERIES that pass through blocks — they get subtracted from block plot_land (a building can't sit on a lane). ROAD-HIERARCHY REWARD: the `- 6*road_near(0.02)` term makes a hop CHEAPER when it runs along an EXISTING road (the city artery, laid before this pass) — so a lane prefers to ride the highway corridor and peel off, instead of cutting its own parallel line. That's the on-ramp/exit read: local roads use the artery as a connector, not a thing to duplicate. Clamped ≥0.05 so cost never goes negative (Dijkstra needs positive edges).
routes.cost: max(0.05, 1 + 8*field(water, fx, fy) + 5*field(steeper, fx, fy) + 10*field(rock, fx, fy) - 6*road_near(0.02))

routes._path_note: ★ BLOCK CONNECTING PATHS with ROAD HIERARCHY (founder: 'a road connecting house A to house B should route from A, SEE the highway, take an exit further down, then reach B — use highways as connection points, not merge into them'). Two data levers do this, no engine change: (1) `gates: true` pulls the artery/lane crossing points into the path net as nodes, so a path can JOIN the bigger road at a real access point; (2) the strong `- 9*road_near(0.015)` reward makes travelling ALONG an existing road far cheaper than cutting a fresh line, so Dijkstra routes A → nearest road → rides it → exits near B. redundancy 1 (was 2) = one honest connection per plot, so roads don't run 50%-overlapped in the same direction (real roads run parallel only occasionally, never half-tracing each other). Clamped ≥0.05 so edges stay positive.
routes.cost: max(0.05, 1 + 10*field(water, fx, fy) + 6*field(steeper, fx, fy) + 8*field(rock, fx, fy) - 11*road_near(0.018))

_templates_note: THE TEMPLATE LAYER — captured compositions, not new machinery. A template is a generator FRAGMENT deep-merged under any generator that names it (the generator's own keys win; nested objects merge). The loader expands it before the engine ever looks; the engine only sees prims. `carved_lots` = the proven 'tile the buildable land honestly' recipe (mask-aware subdivide carve, clipped to the coast) — EXTRACTED from the block and building generators below, which now share it. A dev could define `alien_hive` the same way; nothing here knows what a lot is.

templates._carved_urban_lots_note: SAME carve recipe, STRICTER land: building plots tile `buildable_block` (buildable minus a coast setback minus steeper slope) instead of raw buildable — so buildings sit on tight urban lots set back from the water. Only the clip field differs; the machinery is identical. A dev picks the mask, the engine carves it.
templates._carved_plot_lots_note: THE ROAD/PLOT RECONCILIATION (founder: 'how can you plop a building OVER a road?'). Building plots carve from `plot_land` = buildable_block MINUS the district ARTERIES running through the block (`road_near`). The through-roads are cut OUT of the land, so nothing builds on them; the GAPS between the remaining subdivide plots ARE the local streets (construction determines block roads — no separately-routed block street network, that double-drew roads fighting the plots). Two directions of causality: ABOVE the block road→land (artery cuts buildable); INSIDE the block land→road (plots make streets).

_planet_laws_note: PLANET LAWS (bestiary vocabulary — same names + directions as worlds/bestiary.json, so one address describes ONE planet across every page). Terra is the DEFAULT world; here the laws are constants (the fallback), but composeSpecFor (temple/templates.ts) OVERRIDES them per-address from bodyLaws() so no two addresses roll the same life. `heat` is the PLANET BASELINE temperature (is this world survivable un-suited at all) — a cold planet grows fur everywhere; local district warmth (below) varies AROUND this baseline, so a hot planet's coolest quarter is still hot. `air` breeds flyers (thin air → more, `clamp(1.35-1.55*air)`), `gravity` caps size + low-slungs striders, `lush` sets herd size. These flow city→district so a fauna reads `parent.air`/`parent.heat` etc. — the exact soul /bestiary.html draws.

generators._note: FAUNA — wildlife as data, and the SAME GENOME the /bestiary.html portrait draws (the 'one soul at every LOD' law). A critter's STATS are its species; every BODY-PLAN trait is a golden-ratio hash of (species, district `gene`) BENT BY THE PLANET'S LAWS (parent.air/gravity/heat/lush, flowed city→district above) — verbatim the formulas in worlds/bestiary.json. So all members of a species in a region look/act alike, species differ, the SAME species reads differently in the next region (regional variation for free), and thin air breeds flyers / heavy gravity low-slungs striders / cold grows fur — provably, across the planet. The renderer stamps these with design/creature.ts drawSilhouette (the exact fn the bestiary portrait composes), so the in-world critter and its codex portrait are literally one genome. Wooded/grassy districts teem; dense stone ones are sparse. Births spawn-inherit (a calf IS its species), so demography is real and extinction is permanent (see probe_hunt).
generators._count_note: FAUNA COUNT scales by life_density (the engine-only MAGNITUDE flowed from the orbital fact sheet, city→district). A teeming world (density 1) spawns the full biome-driven count; a sparse hidden-life world (density ~0.1) spawns a handful across many districts — some districts empty — so FINDING the creature is the gameplay on a world the scan called dead. Base Veranholm density=1, unchanged. The +0.4 floor on the multiplier keeps a living world from rounding to a ghost town, while a genuinely near-zero density still empties most districts.

coverage.rock: or(field(rock, fx, fy), field(steeper, fx, fy))

generators._child_stats_note: child_stats evaluate TOP-TO-BOTTOM against the child, so later formulas read earlier stats by name (angle/radius/spread → cx/cy → elev). No repeated sub-expressions. TWO TEMPERATURES, deliberately distinct: `warmth` is LOCAL urban comfort (downtown runs hotter, the fringe cooler) and it varies AROUND the planet baseline `parent.heat` — so a scorching planet's coolest quarter is still hot, a temperate one has a mild-to-warm spread; the settlement reads warmth (busy, HUD, district hue, weather diffusion). `heat`/`air`/`gravity`/`lush` flow city→district under bestiary's OWN names so the FAUNA genome reads parent.heat/parent.air exactly as worlds/bestiary.json does (one address = one planet across every page). `heat` is the whole-planet baseline the fur law reads, NOT the urban warmth.

generators._treatment: THE CITY TREATMENT (the missing middle level): a district partitions into BLOCKS that HONESTLY tile its buildable land — Voronoi seeds scattered ON the land (`where`), cells clipped to the coast (`clip`), same recipe as districts. Contiguous land-following parcels, no overlap. Local STREETS route between them. A district is a small city.

generators._count_note: BUILDING COUNT ∝ the block's real LAND (`area` = carved buildable fraction). Because the carve splits the land among exactly `count` lots, fewer count = BIGGER lots — so this is ALSO the min-lot-size lever (founder: 'improve min size'). area*65: a sliver block (area~0.02) → ~2 big buildings, a roomy block (area~0.1) → ~8. + a small busy nudge. Clamped 1..14. Raise the 65 for finer grain / more smaller lots, lower it for fewer bigger lots.
generators._treatment: THE CITY TREATMENT, ONE SCALE DOWN: buildings are PLOTS that honestly tile the block's PLOT land (`carved_plot_lots` clips to `plot_land` = buildable_block minus the district arteries running through — so buildings never sit on a road, and the gaps between plots ARE the local streets). Same partition prim as districts — a block is a smaller city, resolved through its own world patch automatically. The carve writes each plot's real land fraction to `area` (see the `footprint` rule below — building SIZE ⊕ its honest lot).

generators._person_note: ★ PEOPLE (TEMPLATE #2 — person + schedule). Spawned at BLOCK level so they populate the street you dive into (parallel to buildings). No cascade: a person is Coarse until its block is the focus, but is_active() only checks ANCESTORS — so when you dive to this block (city→district→block all Detailed) its people come alive and live their day; fold the block and they freeze. That's the LOD bargain applied to citizens. ONE person kind (the engine doesn't know what a 'being' is — a dev's enemies/NPCs are just entities with different stats); `resident` is a TAG, not a separate kind. Count ∝ the block's real land + busyness, roughly tracking its homes. The room `occupant` (deeper) stays as interior-zoom detail until movement/rendering unifies them. Renders once the pawn pass lands; testable now via worlds/citylife.json.

generators._venue_note: ★ VENUES (templates #3 + #4). Draw-places on the block's nightlife — count ∝ how busy the block is, so quiet residential blocks may have none and lively ones a few. Each carries its own EVOLVING reputation (`appeal`, starts with a small random spread so no two are identical, then drifts with performance via the rule) plus a nightly hidden food_quality and a stock of mains that depletes when packed. The person head_out/stay_out actions and the venue rules/events make the emergent nightlife proven in citylife (busy-feedback, word-of-mouth, run-dry migration). Active only while the block is Detailed (dived) — LOD-honest.

generators._natural_note: ★ NATURAL DETAIL ON REVEAL — the LOD payoff. When you DIVE INTO a block, the land itself opens into features, not just buildings. A `grove` is a real child entity (like a building): cx/cy start as random scatter, then the partition's `where` gate PULLS each grove onto WOODED ground, sampled through the block's OWN patch (fractal) — so groves land exactly where the forest biome is inside this block. count ∝ how forest-y the DISTRICT is (parent.wooded_frac) × this block's real land (area). We read the DISTRICT's fraction, not the block's, ON PURPOSE: coverage runs at the district carve (voronoi+clip), whereas the block is a subdivide carve that skips coverage AND whose settle runs AFTER this generator — so the district value is the one that's actually populated when this fires (verified against write_canon order). Frozen to canon at reveal: deterministic per seed, persistent — the same trees every time you return. CASCADE: true → each grove immediately reveals its own trees (below), so at block zoom a grove resolves into REAL INDIVIDUAL TREES, not a blob. `capacity` seeds how many trees the grove holds; the grove's live stats then ROLL UP from those trees (see rollups) — chop a tree and the grove thins by itself.

generators._tree_note: ★★ INDIVIDUAL TREES — the finest terrain LOD, and REAL ENTITIES so the sim can act on them. Each tree is a child of its grove, scattered in a small radius AROUND the grove's world position (parent.cx/cy are FINAL here — the grove's where-relocation ran before this cascade-reveal). A tree carries `health`, `age`, `species`. Because trees are entities, `despawn` on a tree = CHOP IT DOWN: the grove's rollups (below) re-aggregate next tick, so tree_n drops, density falls, and the grove can die when its last tree is gone — deforestation as pure data, no special case. The renderer draws each tree as a real trunk+crown stamp at block zoom; count/health of the grove are just its trees summed.

generators._deadgrass_note: ★ DEAD-GRASS PATCHES — the same mechanism for decay/dryness. Scattered onto the block's DRY / parched-grassy land (where-gated to `dry` OR `grassy`), count ∝ the district's dry+grassy character (parent.*_frac, populated at the district carve). Wealthy blocks tend their land, so `wealth` trims the count — a data lever, not a renderer hack. Each patch is a child entity with a `size`; the renderer tints those cells toward a sere yellow-brown. Reads as 'this corner has gone to seed', straight from the moisture field one scale finer.

partition.where: or(field(dry, fx, fy), field(grassy, fx, fy))

generators._boulder_note: ★ BOULDERS — the COMPLEMENT to trees: features claim DIFFERENT terrain. Trees take the woods; boulders take the bare ROCK and STEEP slopes where trees can't grow (where-gated to rock OR steeper). Fills the grey mountain ground that read as empty. Count ∝ how craggy the district is (a rock_frac coverage on the district) × block land. A boulder is a single scatter entity (no children — a rock isn't a forest); the renderer stamps a grey stone with a lit facet. Proves the SAME scatter recipe generalizes to the opposite biome — the demo's whole pitch.

partition.where: or(field(rock, fx, fy), field(steeper, fx, fy))

generators._bloom_note: ★ WILDFLOWER PATCHES — a PATCH entity (like deadgrass), NOT counted flowers: one bloom patch, the renderer stipples colourful dabs WITHIN it. Scattered on grassy meadow (where-gated to grassy). Count ∝ the district's grassy character, and WEALTH raises it (tended land blooms; the same meaning→looks lever, opposite sign from deadgrass). `hue` picks the patch's flower colour family so different meadows bloom differently.

partition.where: and(field(gentle, fx, fy), not(field(wooded, fx, fy)))

generators._reed_note: ★ REEDS — thin marsh growth at the WATER'S EDGE, on the shore/beach band you can now see at block zoom. where-gated to `beach` (calm shore, not cliff). A reed clump is a scatter entity; the renderer draws a few vertical strokes. Finishes the shore transition — land → reeds → shallows → water.

generators._riverrock_note: ★ RIVER ROCKS — small stones IN the shallow water at the land's edge (where-gated to `shallows` = water near land). Unlike trees, rocks DO belong in water — they break up the flat blue channels the eye follows. A scatter entity; the renderer stamps a small wet stone that sits ON the animated water (marked so the water doesn't overpaint it).

generators._room_note: ROOM COUNT scales with the building's CLASS and wealth — a home is 3-6 rooms, a shop floor is fewer/larger (retail + back), an office is an open count. `use` threads down so the interior renderer knows the class. door-chained: the engine's real adjacency the occupants walk.

generators._decor_note: ★ PROCEDURAL DECOR AS LAZY CANON (founder: 'procedural decor entities — until observed, then canon'). Decor spawns when its ROOM is first REVEALED (walk in / open the floor) — before that the room is only an idea; after, the furniture is a written fact, same pieces every revisit. `type` picks from the class's furniture set (renderer maps use×type → stamp); dx/dy are room-local placement the renderer respects. Count scales with room size.

rules._note: fauna metabolism + roaming. Herbivores (diet<=0.72) graze back food each tick; predators burn it and must hunt. Roam = a small deterministic drift scaled by the species' gait.

rules._clock_note: ★ THE CLOCK (TEMPLATE #1) — the one stat everything reads: schedules, venue hours, and the renderer's day/night grade all key off city.hour, so the sim and the render share ONE truth (per TEMPLATE_CATALOG §0). `on:city` runs EVERY tick at EVERY zoom — the city has no ancestors, so is_active() is always true (verified in world.rs) — so time flows whether you're at city view or dived into a room. RATE = 0.25 h/tick → a full day ≈ 96 ticks; raise it for faster days. Hour has no modulo in the DSL, so it wraps via the day-rollover EVENT below (the proven probe_time payday pattern: reset on the rising edge). is_night/is_work are derived here so any scope can gate on them once broadcast down.

rules._person_needs_note: ★ NEEDS DECAY (template #2, proven in worlds/citylife.json) — energy drains while awake, hunger climbs, social fades; the schedule actions replenish them. `food` = 1-hunger for readouts/HUD. These only run when the person's block is Detailed (dived) — the LOD bargain.

rules._fun_note: ★ FUN (templates #3/#4, proven in citylife) — while at a venue, your fun = the food you were SERVED (0.9) + the buzz of the COMPANY (0.1), both read via edge_mean over the guest edge; off the edge it decays. Feeds the venue's word AND your own stay-or-go, so a bad plate or a burnt-out room lowers fun and you drift off.

rules._walk_note: ★ WALKING (movement, respecting time) — the schedule (the `at` the actions set THIS tick) names WHERE you should be: at work → your job spot, out at a venue → that venue's spot (read through the guest edge), otherwise home. Then px/py step toward that target by `spd` each tick — actual travel, in data, no pathfinding: people flow to work by day, to the venues at night, home to sleep. The renderer draws the live px/py, so you watch the street fill and empty with the clock.

rules._dist_note: live distance from where you ARE to your job — the actions read it to know whether you've arrived (earn) or still need to travel (commute).

rules._venue_web_note: ★ VENUES (templates #3 + #4, the emergent nightlife proven in worlds/citylife.json + `dive`). is_open from the block's broadcast hour; crowd = guests linked now; served = food_quality while stock lasts then scraps; word = mean fun of who's here (info that only forms once people are in). appeal_now = a DYNAMIC reputation (`appeal`, evolves below) + word + a WORD-GATED crowd pull (a crowd only draws while it's having a good time, so a burnt-out room stops pulling → migration) + a noise floor outside is_open (no 0-vs-0 opening tie). REPUTATION drifts day by day: a busy venue whose guests have fun (word>0.5) climbs, a busy dud fades — so even the pecking order is emergent, not authored.

rules._vitality_note: ★ DISTRICT VITALITY (0..1) — the macro health that decides whether the whole district is being kept up. Starts healthy (an existing, living community) and stays high while the district thrives; drops when the place FAILS (a stat like warmth/wealth collapsing, or an authored decline event). A district whose vitality craters loses upkeep across ALL its plots AT ONCE → people-sparse → work decays → buildings overgrow → plots revert. The failing-city → wilderness cascade, from one coarse dial. Gentle drift toward a wealth-driven target so a poor district is more fragile; wire an event to slam it to 0 to watch a district die. Runs coarse (per-tick, every LOD) so decline is real even at city zoom.

rules._block_vitality_note: vitality PROJECTED onto the block, because a BUILDING's parent is its BLOCK (building→block→district), so a building's `upkeep` rule reads `parent.vitality` = the BLOCK's copy. The block just mirrors its district's vitality down one level (parent.vitality here = the district's). One hop so the plot-level gate can read it as parent.vitality.

rules._work_live_note: ★ THE LIVING PLOT (per-tick). `work` (0 wild → 1 fully worked) is SUSTAINED by occupancy and a healthy district, and DECAYS toward wild when neglected — nature reclaims unkept land. UPKEEP = is this plot occupied / its community alive: souls here, or the district still has life. When upkeep holds, work drifts UP to a sustain ceiling; with no upkeep it DECAYS, and the rate depends on `surface` (paved 0.004/tick resists longest, lawn 0.010, bare dirt 0.020 — concrete outlasts a mowed lawn outlasts turned dirt). This is why you can clear a plot, wander 800 miles, and return to find it overgrown; and why a district whose population fails goes people-sparse → buildings decay → plots revert, all from ONE stat. Deterministic, per-tick, no engine change — pure rule + rollup.

rules._upkeep_note: UPKEEP presence: is this plot's community keeping it up. CRITICAL LOD POINT: occupants aren't REVEALED at block zoom (floors/occupants materialize only on deeper dive — that's the whole LOD lever), so upkeep can NOT read revealed `souls`. It reads the plot's OWN `occupancy` (a settle stat: is this an inhabited plot of the existing community) gated by district vitality (`parent.vitality`, 0..1). So a lived-in plot in a healthy district is kept up (work holds, structure stands); an empty/frontier plot, or ANY plot once its district's vitality craters, loses upkeep → work decays → reclaim. The macro decline cascade lives in `vitality` (below), one dial dropping upkeep across every plot at once. This is upkeep at the COARSE scale — honest to the LOD, no occupant reveal required.

rules._standing_note: ★ DOES A STRUCTURE STAND (per-tick, live). Eligible (capacity>0) AND fully worked (work≥1) → a building stands and LATCHES up. If work later decays below a dilapidation floor (0.55), the structure can no longer be maintained → standing drops → the plot overgrows. So 'can't build until fully worked' AND 'a neglected building decays back to land' are the SAME gate, read live. The renderer draws a roof only where standing>0.5; below that it's yard/tamed/wild by `work`.

rules._shop_econ_note: ★ SHOP STOCK & PRICE (template #5, live). Stock restocks at the shop's `supply` rate and drains with local DEMAND (a busy block empties shelves faster). PRICE = scarcity: falls as stock piles up, rises when it's bare, and lifts with neighbourhood WEALTH — so the same good is dearer uptown, and a run on a shop shows up as a price spike. This is the substrate a MERCHANT reads (buy where cheap, sell where dear). Only shops (use==1) participate; runs only while the block is Detailed.

rules._grove_established_note: LATCH: once a grove has ever held trees (tree_n > 0, set by the tree→grove rollup), `established` sticks at 1. The clear-cut event reads this so it can't misfire on the reveal tick (before the first rollup, tree_n is still 0 but the grove isn't empty — it just hasn't aggregated yet). max() makes the latch monotonic.

rules._note: WEATHER as DATA (not engine code). Each district samples the `cloud` FIELD at its own location and ACCUMULATES it as cloud_density (a slow build toward the local cloud level). RULES (not events) drive this + `raining`, because at the city zoom districts are COARSE/dormant and events only run on ACTIVE (revealed) scopes — but rules run coarse too, so weather state stays live at every LOD. `raining` = a hysteresis band (on above 0.66, off below 0.5) so it doesn't flicker at the threshold. The broadcast pushes it to children so zooming in can SHOW rain.

_settle_note: ★ WORLDGEN, NOT SIM — the founder's crucial correction: 'saturation determines things BEFORE we look at the zoom level, not during.' Land use + zoning saturation + footprint are DECIDED ONCE, when a scope is first observed (reveal), iterated to convergence, then FROZEN to canon. They live in `settle_rules` (run at reveal), NOT `rules` (run every tick) — so buildings can NEVER bounce as the sim runs, because nothing recomputes them after reveal. This is the double-slit / lazy-canon: observation crystallizes the possibility into a fixed fact. Because blocks reveal lazily (on dive), each building's use is fixed when its block is revealed, and its civic count flows UP to the district (settle_ancestors) so a later-dived block sees the district already saturated — cross-block scarcity accumulates as you explore. No hysteresis / confirmation-counter needed anymore: settle converges once, cleanly, then never runs again.

settle_rules._capacity_note: ★ LOT SIZE → CAPACITY (founder: 'couldn't we make lot size determine building capacity/availability just as easily?'). Lots come first (count ∝ block area, so they emerge from the LAND); then each lot's SIZE decides what it holds. `capacity` = how much this lot supports: a min-area GATE (area < ~0.004 → capacity 0 = EMPTY, a yard/pocket, NO building drawn — this is 'min size' as a real gate, not a crammed sliver), then rising with area. Everything downstream (does it build, footprint, split) reads capacity. THE model: the land decides, not a count formula.

settle_rules._built_note: does this lot BUILD at all? capacity 0 (too-small lot) → 0 = empty yard. The renderer draws nothing for built=0 (just the lot/ground shows).

settle_rules._footprint_note: BUILDING SIZE ⊕ ITS HONEST LOT — from the lot's `area` (sqrt so small lots still read, capped). Worldgen: fixed when observed. Only matters if built.

settle_rules._situation_note: PLOT SITUATION — road_access = is this plot near a lane (sampled at its world centre via near_road). Fixed at reveal; the use scores below read it.

settle_rules._use_pref_note: USE BY PREFERENCE (founder's 'a shop wants X, a hospital wants big+highway'). Each use SCORES the plot over its situation with a MIN-AREA GATE (ge(area,k)→0 below the size it needs). Highest score claims the plot (argmax below). Resi = strong default baseline (city is mostly homes); the others must earn their niche. Indices: 0 resi 1 shop 2 café 3 clinic 4 school 5 office. Civic scores subtract the DISTRICT saturation (parent.*_sat) — the scarcity feedback, now converging DURING settle (settle iterates rules+rollups so the count→saturation→suppression loop settles before freezing). No hysteresis term needed — settle isn't a per-tick loop, so there's nothing to flicker.

settle_rules._clinic_note: clinic NICHE = road-accessible, wealthy, MID-size (oversize penalty sends big lots to office/school). `- 0.85*parent.clinic_sat` = the district scarcity feedback.

settle_rules._use_argmax_note: argmax → the committed use, written ONCE at settle and frozen. No candidate/confirmation dance — settle converges then stops, so `use` is canon.

settle_rules._flags_note: presence flags → rolled up (building→block→district) so the district saturation counts them. Run in settle so the count feeds back DURING crystallization.

settle_rules._fill_note: ★ ROOFTOP FILL — how much of its LOT a building's roof covers, DECIDED FROM MEANING (founder: 'some fill to the brim, some don't; a rich person keeps most of it yard'). WEALTH lowers fill (the rich keep lawn/setback); BUSY raises it (dense commercial fills the brim); USE matters — shops/offices/clinic pack their lots (0.9+), apartments moderate, schools sit in grounds (low fill, big yard). area nudges (a huge lot keeps proportionally more open). All authorable: change the formula to change the city's grain. 0.3..0.95 of the carved lot shape. This is worldgen (settle), frozen.

settle_rules._split_note: ★ LOT SPLIT — a big lot can hold MORE THAN ONE building (founder: 'some might split a lot to 2 buildings'). split = how many footprints on this lot: big + dense residential/mixed splits into 2 (a duplex / two houses on a wide lot); very big → 3; small or single-purpose (civic, shops) stays 1. The renderer draws `split` footprints across the lot. Meaning-driven: raise the area gates for a coarser grain, add uses that subdivide.

settle_rules._surface_note: ★ PLOT SURFACE (settle, frozen) — what the ground of this plot is graded to, which sets how FAST it reverts to wild when neglected. 0 = bare dirt (reverts fastest), 1 = lawn/tamed grass (medium), 2 = paved (reverts slowest — concrete resists nature longest). Commercial/office/civic → paved; homes → lawn; unbuilt/frontier → dirt. Read by the per-tick `work` decay below as a resistance term. Frozen canon like use/fill.

settle_rules._shop_seed_note: ★ THE SHOP ECONOMY (TEMPLATE #5 goods/prices). Only shops (use==1) trade. `supply` = this shop's own restock rate (a per-shop trait, so shops differ → prices differ). `stock` starts varied. Frozen at settle; the live stock/price rules below take over. Non-shops carry stock 0 (they don't price).

settle_rules._work_seed_note: ★ INITIAL WORK LEVEL (settle, frozen — the STARTING point the live `work` stat below decays/sustains from). How developed this plot is WHEN YOU FIND IT: the existing community's plots are already worked-up (you land into a built world), the frontier is half-tamed or wild. Driven by MEANING: an eligible plot (capacity>0) on an established, road-accessible, occupied part of the district starts high; remote/low-occupancy plots start lower. Only buildable land can be worked at all — but every plot here is already on plot_land, so the floor is the meaning, not the mask (the mask already gated the carve). This is the frozen SEED; `work` (per-tick) takes it live.

settle_rules._work_init_note: SEED the live `work` stat to `work_seed` at reveal so the per-tick rule (in `rules`) has a starting value to decay/sustain from (a bare per-tick `work = f(work,…)` would start at 0). max() makes it a one-way latch at settle — settle iterates, but work only ever gets seeded UP to work_seed here; the live decay owns it after.

settle_rules._recipe_note: ★ SCALE-SEEDED SCARCITY (reusable recipe): 'a scarce thing suppresses MORE of itself at the scale above.' clinics per district = legendary loot per dungeon = one blacksmith per town. Presence flag → rollup count up the tree → ancestor → saturation → suppress. Now settled at WORLDGEN time (once, at reveal), not per-tick — so it can't bounce. Because settle iterates rules+rollups to convergence, the saturation is a plain clamped target (no damping needed — there's no per-tick oscillation when it converges once and freezes).

settle_rules._sat_down_note: SATURATION FLOWS DOWN one level per rule (block reads its district; buildings read their block via parent.*_sat in the scores). Each is the sole writer on its kind → no clobber. In settle these run top-down each pass, so the district's saturation reaches buildings within the convergence.

settle_rules._tree_viable_note: ★★ TREES RESPECT THE TERRAIN — the same way a building respects its lot. A tree scatters in a ring around its grove; that ring can spill onto RIVER, bare ROCK, or a ROAD. Rather than a renderer band-aid (draw-skip), we settle it into STATE: sample the fields at the tree's own world spot (wx/wy, threaded down through the grove's block patch) and decide whether it actually GREW there. water → never (0); a swept road corridor → never (0, trees DODGE roads exactly like plots do via road_near); rock/steep → rare hardy survivor (chance(0.12), deterministic per tree); otherwise it lives. This writes `alive`, the SAME stat the tree→grove rollup sums — so an unviable tree is simply not one of the grove's trees: tree_n counts 2/3/4 real trees exactly like a block counts its real buildings, no phantom trunks in the water or on the tarmac. Frozen at reveal (settle), deterministic. The `where`-gate keeps most trees on wooded land; this culls the ring-scatter spillover it can't reach (it only relocates the grove seed, not each tree).
settle_rules.expr: iff(gt(field(water, wx, wy), 0.5), 0, iff(gt(field(on_road, wx, wy), 0.5), 0, iff(or(field(rock, wx, wy), field(steeper, wx, wy)), chance(0.12), 1)))

rollups._note: living fauna per district — blocks/venues contribute 0 (no alive stat). Gates births so herds self-cap without counting non-fauna siblings.

COUNT the civic uses up the scale tree, building → block → district. Two hops: buildings sum into their block, blocks sum into their district. `sum` reducer. The district's *_total is the whole-district civic count that drives the saturation (feedback) above.

rollups._grove_rollup_note: ★★ TREES → GROVE (the coupling that makes chopping matter). Every tick each grove RE-AGGREGATES from its live tree children: tree_n = how many trees remain, canopy = their mean health, vigor = same (so a stressed grove reads stressed). Because a `despawn`ed tree simply isn't in the child set next tick, chopping a tree makes tree_n fall and canopy drop with ZERO special-case code — the grove is DEFINED as its trees. An event (below) despawns a grove once tree_n hits 0: clear-cut land goes back to bare ground. This is the bidirectional loop — grove→trees on reveal (worldgen), trees→grove every tick (rollup).

_broadcast_removed_note: We do NOT broadcast the saturation. A broadcast fires at EVERY parent→child pair unconditionally, so it clobbers (city→district would zero the district's computed value; a second broadcast level re-clobbers). Instead the saturation flows down via single-level `parent.X` RULES (below): a block copies its district's clinic_sat, a building reads its block's — each rule is the sole writer of that stat on that kind, so no clobber. Cleaner than broadcast for a computed value that must survive. RECIPE NOTE: to push a COMPUTED scope stat downward, prefer a `set X = parent.X` rule per level over a broadcast.

actions._note: fauna behaviors — what the codex OBSERVES. Predators stalk the smallest co-located prey (@min pick on size, rank zeroes non-fauna since their alive=0); grazing tops herbivores up; wandering is the default. last_action + these labels are the codex's raw material.
actions.name: hunt

actions._schedule_note: ★ THE SCHEDULE (template #2, tuned in worlds/citylife.json) — utility AI gated on the CLOCK broadcast down to this block (parent.is_night / parent.is_work), NOT scheduled-at-time events (the ⚠ untested prim). The highest score wins each tick, so the routine EMERGES: sleep pulls at night (harder when tired), work is the daytime plurality with a soft energy term, eat spikes with hunger, socialising fills the evenings, the break spikes when exhausted. `at`: 0 home · 1 work · 2 out · 3 eatery — the location the pawn-render pass will read to walk them.
actions.name: sleep

actions._towork_note: ★ LEAVE FOR WORK — ANTICIPATORY, EMERGENT (respecting time). Nobody says 'go now': the person fires this once the clock passes their OWN leave_h = 9 − (commute ÷ walk-speed) − diligence. So a person whose job is across the block leaves EARLIER than one next door (longer commute), and a diligent soul who loves the job leaves earlier still (the diligence buffer) while a slacker cuts it fine. Setting at=1 makes the walk-rule carry them toward the job; they don't EARN until they arrive (the work action needs dist_job≈0). Two people, same 9am start, leave at different times and arrive early / on-time / late — purely from their distance and their trait.
actions.name: to_work

actions._work_note: ★ WORK — only EARN once you've actually arrived (dist_job small). So the commute is real: leave late and you clock in late, losing the early hours' wage. at=1 keeps you at your post.
actions.name: work

actions._headout_note: ★ HEAD OUT (template #3) — pick a venue ONCE (gated lt(edge_count(guest),1), so no per-tick re-pick → no sloshing). `link guest:venue@max:target.appeal_now` grabs the highest-appeal open venue among the block's venues; the 0.55 base keeps you wanting out after a burnt-out venue empties, so you re-pick next door (migration). Blocks with no venue → the link finds nothing and you just stay in / idle.
actions.name: head_out

actions._stayout_note: ★ STAY OUT — commitment (kills sloshing) + the exit valve: score rises with FUN, so a good night holds you and a bad/dry one lets sleep or a break outbid → you leave, maybe to the other venue. `affect guest:stock -0.14` = you eat, drawing the venue's mains down (the busiest venue burns through fastest → its own scarcity).
actions.name: stay_out

events._note: fauna demography — the probe_hunt pattern, slower rates for a persistent world. Births spawn-inherit: a calf carries its parent's species/traits with age reset, so lineages are real and extinction is permanent.
events.label: a calf is born

events._dayroll_note: ★ DAY ROLLOVER (clock template) — resets the hour accumulator and counts the day. Rising-edge on ge(hour,24): fires once at midnight, the `do` subtracts 24 (which re-arms the edge for tomorrow) and increments day. Modulo-free periodic pattern (probe_time payday). Runs on the always-active city, so days accrue at every zoom.
events.label: 🌅 a new day breaks over Veranholm

events._dusk_note: Chronicle colour + the render's cue to warm the grade. Band rising-edge (fires once in the 20:00–21:00 window, re-arms after midnight) so it beats exactly once per evening.
events.label: 🌆 dusk settles over the city

events._venue_events_note: ★ NIGHTLIFE BEATS (templates #3/#4). The nightly ROLL sets a fresh hidden food_quality + restocks the mains at opening (rand differs each night). The GATHERING fires when a venue fills. The STOCKOUT beat when the mains run dry (consequence of being too popular → migration). All rising-edge, re-arm each night; only fire on active (dived) blocks.
events.label: 🍳 opens for the night
🎉 the place fills up — a scene kicks off
🍽 runs out of the main course

events._clearcut_note: ★★ A GROVE DIES WHEN ITS LAST TREE IS CHOPPED. Guarded by `established` (a rule sets it to 1 once the grove has ever had trees), so this can't misfire on the reveal tick before the first tree→grove rollup runs (when tree_n is still 0). Rising-edge: fires ONCE, the frame tree_n falls to 0 on an established grove → despawn the now-empty grove so clear-cut land reverts to bare ground. Chopping is `despawn` on a tree (a host intent / future action); the grove's death is pure consequence — no code knows what a 'forest' is.
events.label: grove cleared

broadcasts._note: Push the district's rain state DOWN to its children (blocks) so zooming in can render rain in the areas where it's actually raining. gain 1 = copy the parent value straight down.

broadcasts._clock_down_note: ★ CLOCK DOWNWARD (template #2 needs this) — the clock lives on the city; a person's schedule reads parent.is_night / parent.is_work where parent = its block. Broadcasts are one level, so relay it city→district then district→block. gain 1 = straight copy. On a dive the whole path is Detailed, so the current clock reaches the people you're watching.

broadcasts._hour_to_venues_note: relay hour one more level, block→children, so venues can gate their own open-hours (template #3).
